Ecoscope · eden-community_engagement

Community Engagement Workflow

Community engagement is central to conservation success. Protected area managers and rangers hold regular meetings with local communities to share information, resolve conflicts, gather intelligence, and build trust. This workflow automates tracking that process. It connects to your EarthRanger site, pulls your community meeting events, and produces a dashboard plus a set of Word reports showing who you're reaching, how participation is trending, which topics are coming up, and where meetings are concentrated across the landscape. The dashboard and reports are polished enough to share as-is, with a donor or funding partner, not just for internal tracking.

Before you begin, you'll need

  • An EarthRanger account for the site you want to track, with a username and password.
1

Workflow Template

In the navigation bar, click Workflow Templates. On the Select a Workflow Template page, click the Add Workflow Template button in the top right corner of that page.

Paste in the workflow's GitHub link:

https://github.com/wildlife-dynamics/eden-community_engagement

Then click Add Template. Community Engagement is now added to your workflow library, ready for you to configure and run against your own EarthRanger data.

2

Data Source

Click Data Sources in the navigation bar, then click Connect Data Source in the top right.

Fill in:

  • Data Source Name, a name for this connection, e.g. Olokeri
  • EarthRanger URL, your site's subdomain, e.g. cc-olokeri (the .pamdas.org part is added automatically)
  • EarthRanger Username and EarthRanger Password, your login credentials

Then click Connect.

Heads up: your credentials aren't checked when you hit Connect. If something's wrong, you won't find out until you actually try running a workflow against it.

3

Open Your Workflow

Find the eden-community_engagement card under My Workflows and click it. This opens its configuration form, starting with the details below.

Now that setup is done

Workflow Configuration

The rest of this guide walks through the workflow's own configuration form, section by section, in the order it appears on the page.

1

Workflow Details

  • Workflow Name — required. Identifies this run in My Workflows and at the top of its dashboard, worth naming by site if you plan to run the same template against more than one data source, for example Community Engagement, Olokeri.
  • Workflow Description — optional free-text note for yourself or a teammate; neither field is read by any task in the workflow itself.
2

Data Source

Select the EarthRanger connection you added earlier, under Data Sources. This is what tells the workflow which site's meetings to pull, so it needs to match the connection you just set up. Every other setting further down this form stays the same regardless of which site you choose here, the sites themselves aren't identical underneath (different required fields, different naming for things like the meeting convener), but the workflow already accounts for that, so you don't have to.

3

Define Base Maps

Expand Advanced Configurations to see the base map stack, a list of tile layers drawn under the choropleth map later in the form. Layers stack in the order listed, the first one is the bottommost. It defaults to two: Terrain at full opacity, with Satellite on top at 50% opacity, so administrative boundaries stay visible under the imagery instead of being hidden by it.

Use the up/down arrows to reorder layers, the trash icon to remove one, or Add to add another.

4

Time Range

Sets the window of events this run analyzes, and the timezone every timestamp is interpreted in, including any Quarter/Semester grouping set up in the next section.

Quarter labels like 2026 Q3 are computed directly from calendar months in this timezone, not a fiscal calendar, so it's worth matching Timezone to the site's actual locale (Africa/Nairobi for Olokeri) rather than leaving a default.

5

Set Groupers

Determines how the dashboard splits into separate, switchable views. Leave it blank and everything lands in one view.

  • Group by — chooses Time (buckets by calendar period: Quarter, Semester, Month, Year, day of week, and more) or Category (buckets by a field already pulled from the meeting data, either the location or the meeting convener).
  • Category — only appears once Group by is set to Category, and picks which field to split on.

Add more than one grouper and their splits combine, Quarter and Meeting Convener together produce one view per quarter-per-convener pair. The choropleth map is the one exception: it silently excludes the Location grouper from re-splitting, since the map is already colored by location.

6

Get Community Engagement Events

Filters which EarthRanger event type counts as a community engagement meeting.

  • Event Types — the literal filter passed to the EarthRanger events query, only events of these type(s) are pulled in at all. Defaults to community_meeting.
  • Advanced Configurations → Force Point Geometry — on by default, collapses any polygon/multipolygon event geometry down to a single centroid point, since the marker layers drawn on the choropleth later expect points rather than shapes.
7

Choropleth Setting

Controls how the meetings-per-location-polygon map is colored. Counts are bucketed using Natural Breaks (Jenks) classification, which finds the largest real gaps in the data rather than splitting it into equal-width bins.

  • Colormap — any sequential matplotlib colormap name.
  • Max Classes — caps how many color bins get created, automatically reduced if fewer distinct values exist in the data.
  • Colormap Min and Colormap Max — trim the sampled range of the colormap (0 to 1); raise Min to skip the palest shades, lower Max to avoid the darkest one reading as almost black.
8

Choropleth View State

Sets the map's rendering viewport, not just cosmetic.

  • Viewport Width and Viewport Height — feed directly into the zoom level used to frame the polygons. Zoom level is what actually determines basemap tile sharpness; a higher screenshot scale factor later doesn't fetch higher-resolution tiles on its own.
  • For the sharpest result, these should match Screenshot Setting's Width/Height exactly (Section 10).

An advanced Padding field (default 0.2) sets how much empty margin is reserved around the polygons when the view auto-fits to them.

9

Event Marker Size & Text Layer Size

Two independent sizing variables, each shared across more than one drawing layer.

  • Event Marker Size (under its own Advanced Configurations, default 5px radius) — sets the size of the meeting-location dot markers; both the normal event layer and the missing-location marker layer share this one value.
  • Text Layer Size (default 12px) — sets the font size for every text label drawn on the map; polygon names, meeting-count labels, and missing-location labels all share this one value too.
10

Screenshot Setting

Controls how the interactive map is captured to a static PNG for the dashboard and the report, a headless-browser screenshot of the same HTML you'd see live.

  • Scale Factor — capped at 2. Pushing it higher forces deck.gl to render at a device-pixel ratio it doesn't tile correctly for, which crops the map rather than sharpening it; 2 is already sharp on retina-class displays.
  • Wait Time — gives slow-loading basemap tiles time to finish painting after the page reports network-idle.
  • Width and Height — should match Choropleth View State's Viewport Width/Height.
  • Full Page — captures the entire scrollable page instead of just the visible viewport. Leave off for the choropleth, it's a single fixed-size map, not a scrolling page, so this has no effect here.
  • Timeout — Playwright's own navigation/screenshot timeout, in ms. 0 disables timeouts entirely, letting a slow render take as long as it needs rather than failing.
  • Max Concurrent Pages — how many of these headless-browser screenshots render in parallel when a grouper produces several maps in one run (e.g. one per quarter). Higher values finish faster but use proportionally more memory and CPU.
  • Serve Local Files — starts a small local server so deck.gl's local file fetches aren't blocked by browser CORS rules.
11

Community Report

Generates the Word (.docx) report, one per group if groupers are set, a single report otherwise.

Template Path points at the .docx template, built with Jinja2, hosted on GitHub. It's already set to the default template, so there's no need to change it unless you have a reason to use a different one.

After you run the workflow

Dashboard & Files

Once the configuration form has been submitted, and the workflow has finished running, here's what you'll see.

Dashboard

The results page opens on the Dashboard tab, showing stat cards and charts for whichever group is currently selected.

  • View — the panel on the left lets you switch between groups (here, Meeting Convener); change it and the whole dashboard updates to that group's numbers and charts.
  • Context — below it, shows the workflow's own metadata: title, description, time range, and time zone.
  • Edit Layout (top right) — lets you reorder, resize, or hide the dashboard's widgets to set up whichever layout works best for you.

Files

Switch to the Files tab to see the generated output for the currently selected group.

  • Files are tied to whichever group is selected in the View panel; change the grouper selection to see a different group's output.
  • Open Results Folder (top right, replaces Edit Layout on this tab) — opens the folder on disk containing every generated output that's been saved, across every group, not just the one currently selected.

That's everything.
